summary: The Court Physician of Luoyang, sent by the Emperor to the 'ailing' Dong Cheng. Reading the grief behind the illness, he bites off a finger to pledge himself and claims Cao Cao's life 'lies in these hands' - the headache tea. Betrayed by Quin Quington, he pours the poison anyway, refuses to taste it, is tortured through beatings and revivals without naming a soul, and - fingers severed, unbowed - bows toward the palace and dashes his head against the steps.
_note: summary was missing for the chapter's martyr

summary: Dong Cheng's servant, caught whispering with the concubine Yun Ying: forty canes and a dungeon. He breaks out by night, carries the conspiracy to Cao Cao's palace - names, meetings, the bitten finger - and stands as the living witness against his master.
The concubine caught whispering with Quin Quington in the garden; spared death at the lady's plea, whipped forty canes - the household grievance that unravels the belt-decree plot.
Named by the informer among the secret meeters; seized and imprisoned with Wang Zifu, Wu Shi, and Wu Zilan, and made to watch Ji Ping's torture without breaking.
Named by the informer and imprisoned with the other three signatories; sits 'on a rug full of needles' through Ji Ping's interrogation.
Sends his Court Physician to the 'ailing' State Uncle - unknowingly arming the plot - and ends the chapter with Cao Cao's advisers convened to discuss his dethronement.
Han-founding strategist cited in Cao Cao's boast: Xun Yu, Xun You, Guo Jia, and Cheng Yu are 'superior to Xiao He and Chen Ping'.
The Han founder's great chancellor, cited in Cao Cao's boast about his own advisers.
Han restoration general cited by Cao Cao: his fighters outshine 'Cen Peng and Ma Wu'.
Han restoration general in Cao Cao's boast, outshone (he claims) by Zhang Liao and Xu Chu.
Mi Heng's measuring stick: he claims Confucius's virtue for himself, and casts Cao Cao as Yang Huo, the man who belittled the sage.
Paired with Confucius in Mi Heng's boast - and in his accusation: making a famous scholar a drummer boy is 'Zang Cang vilifying Mencius'.
The belittler of Confucius, the role Mi Heng assigns Cao Cao in his six-fouls tirade.
The vilifier of Mencius, the second insult in Mi Heng's drummer-boy protest.
The Shang minister revealed to his king in a dream - Kong Rong's allusion when he pleads that Mi Heng's rudeness is mere misdemeanor.
Cited in Mi Heng's boast that he could make his prince the rival of Kings Yao and Shun.
Cited with Shun in Mi Heng's boast of what a properly-employed genius could make of a prince.
Claimed by Cao Cao as one of his secretaries - and rated by Mi Heng as 'a fair armorer'.
Claimed by Cao Cao among the bravest of the brave - and rated by Mi Heng as 'a fair reader of elegy'.

Torn between suitors, he watches Jia Xu rip up Yuan Shao's letter and dismiss his envoy - 'he cannot even tolerate his own brother' - then submits at Xuchang, where Cao Cao takes his hand and begs him to forget 'that little fault of mine'. Made General Who Possesses Prowess.
_note: old 'summary' was the bare note '(duplicate record)'

summary: Sent as envoy to Xiangyang, he wins Jia Xu first and through him Zhang Xiu - the embassy that turns Wancheng's old enemy into Cao Cao's general.
_note: old summary had him merely advising at court; he is the envoy

summary: Recruits the volunteer Ji Ping - who bites off a finger over the girdle decree - then wrecks everything by flogging the servant Quin Quington over a garden dalliance. Summoned to watch Ji Ping's torture, he brazens it out until the search of his bedroom turns up the decree and the signed pledge; he and his whole household are arrested.
_note: old summary stopped at the recruitment

summary: Named by the informer, imprisoned, and paraded before Ji Ping's torture; his and the others' evidence is the lever Cao Cao uses against Dong Cheng.
_note: old summary was a bare conspirator mention

summary: Named by the informer and imprisoned with Wang Zifu, Chong Ji, and Wu Shi as the plot collapses.
_note: old summary was a bare conspirator mention
